# urban growth management

![Mobile Features AB](https://www.studysmarter.co.uk/app/themes/studypress-core-theme/dist/assets/images/ab-test/mobile-header.png)

Urban [growth management](/explanations/architecture/urban-studies-in-architecture/growth-management/) refers to the [strategic planning](/explanations/architecture/urban-studies-in-architecture/strategic-planning/) and regulation of how cities expand and develop, ensuring sustainable and efficient use of resources. It involves implementing policies such as [zoning](/explanations/architecture/urban-studies-in-architecture/zoning/), [green belts](/explanations/architecture/urban-studies-in-architecture/green-belts/), and [urban growth](/explanations/architecture/urban-design-in-architecture/urban-growth/) boundaries to control the spread of [urban sprawl](/explanations/architecture/urban-studies-in-architecture/urban-sprawl/) and protect natural environments. Effective urban growth management aims to [balance](/explanations/architecture/interior-design-in-architecture/balance/) economic development with environmental conservation and quality of life improvements for urban residents.

## Urban Growth Management Definition and Techniques

To navigate the complexities of increasing urbanization, urban [growth management strategies](/explanations/architecture/urban-studies-in-architecture/growth-management-strategies/) play a vital role. These strategies are designed to control the rate and pattern of [urban expansion](/explanations/architecture/urban-design-in-architecture/urban-expansion/), ensuring the development of cities is sustainable, efficient, and equitable.

### Understanding Urban Growth Management

Urban [growth management](/explanations/architecture/urban-studies-in-architecture/growth-management/) is a system of practices and policies aimed at moderating [urban expansion](/explanations/architecture/urban-design-in-architecture/urban-expansion/). It strives to help cities grow in ways that preserve livability and reduce negative environmental impacts. It involves the regulation of land use and the integration of urban services to optimize the way cities expand.

The concept involves several key factors:

**[Urban Growth](/explanations/architecture/urban-design-in-architecture/urban-growth/) Management:** A strategic approach to manage the expansion of urban areas, focusing on sustainable development and efficient use of resources.

Throughout history, urban growth management has evolved significantly. In ancient times, cities were often confined by defensive walls, which inadvertently enforced a [form](/explanations/architecture/interior-design-in-architecture/form/) of growth control. In contrast, today's strategies are driven by technological advances and societal needs, making them far more comprehensive and forward-thinking.

Modern urban growth management incorporates elements like smart city technologies, which utilize data and connectivity to improve the efficiency of [urban systems](/explanations/architecture/urban-design-in-architecture/urban-systems/) and services. These technologies play a crucial role in adapting cities for future challenges, such as climate change and overpopulation.

### Key Urban Growth Management Techniques

Employing a variety of techniques is crucial in managing urban growth effectively. Techniques often differ based on regional needs but share common objectives. Here are some widely-used techniques:

**Example of Urban Growth Management:** Portland, Oregon, is renowned for its urban growth boundary, which has successfully limited outward expansion while promoting density within the city. This approach has maintained its natural landscape and resulted in efficient [public transportation](/explanations/architecture/urban-design-in-architecture/public-transportation/) networks.

### Role of Comprehensive Urban Planning Methods

Comprehensive [urban planning](/explanations/architecture/urban-studies-in-architecture/urban-planning/) is fundamental to effective urban growth management. It involves thorough assessments and long-term strategic plans for the sustainable development of urban areas. Its role is to ensure resources are used efficiently, communities are well-served, and ecosystems are not compromised.

Key elements of comprehensive [urban planning](/explanations/architecture/urban-studies-in-architecture/urban-planning/) include:

By focusing on these principles, urban planners can anticipate challenges and devise solutions that promote resilience and adaptability in urban environments.

### Benefits of Sustainable Architecture Practices

[Sustainable architecture](/explanations/architecture/sustainable-archiqtectural-design/sustainable-architecture/) practices complement urban growth management by reducing resource consumption and environmental impact. These practices focus on designing buildings and infrastructure in [harmony](/explanations/architecture/interior-design-in-architecture/harmony/) with nature.

The benefits of [sustainable architecture](/explanations/architecture/sustainable-archiqtectural-design/sustainable-architecture/) include:

By adopting sustainable architecture, cities not only reduce their carbon footprint but also enhance quality of life for residents.

Consider incorporating renewable energy sources, such as solar panels, into building designs to further enhance sustainability.

## Urban Growth Management Strategies

Managing urban growth effectively requires a blend of strategic approaches and planning methods. These strategies ensure that as cities expand, they do so in a manner conducive to sustainability and quality of life for all residents.

### Strategic Approaches to Managing Urban Growth

Strategic approaches are crucial in directing [urban development](/explanations/architecture/urban-studies-in-architecture/urban-development/). These approaches include setting clear goals and objectives for urban expansion, closely monitoring growth patterns, and applying policies that guide development in desired directions.

Key strategies include:

Effective management also entails anticipating future challenges and adjusting plans accordingly to maintain resilience and efficiency in [urban systems](/explanations/architecture/urban-design-in-architecture/urban-systems/).

**Example:** Singapore's integrated land-use planning incorporates [public housing](/explanations/architecture/urban-studies-in-architecture/public-housing/), industry, and [green spaces](/explanations/architecture/architectural-analysis/green-spaces/), creating a balanced [urban environment](/explanations/architecture/urban-studies-in-architecture/urban-environment/) that minimizes commuting times.

### Effective Planning with Comprehensive Urban Planning Methods

Comprehensive urban planning is a detailed approach to designing [urban spaces](/explanations/architecture/urban-design-in-architecture/urban-spaces/) that addresses various needs such as [housing](/explanations/architecture/urban-studies-in-architecture/housing/), transportation, and public services. By implementing comprehensive plans, cities can build frameworks that prevent inefficient or haphazard development.

Effective methods involve:

These methods facilitate thoughtful development that harmonizes various urban elements, enhancing the potential for sustainable living practices.

Engage local communities early in the planning process to foster a sense of ownership and improve project outcomes.

### Sustainable Practices in Urban Growth Management

Sustainability is a core objective in urban growth management. Sustainable practices ensure that urban development meets present needs without compromising future generations. Such practices focus on minimizing environmental impacts while maximizing economic and social benefits.

Key sustainable practices include:

By prioritizing sustainable practices, cities can create environments that support economic growth while preserving natural resources and improving the quality of [urban life](/explanations/architecture/urban-design-in-architecture/urban-life/).

**Deep Dive:** The incorporation of [green roofs](/explanations/architecture/environmental-design/green-roofs/) and [vertical gardens](/explanations/architecture/landscape-design/vertical-gardens/) is an innovative sustainable practice that helps urban areas reduce heat, improve air quality, and provide additional green space, which enhances urban biodiversity.

## Impact of Urban Growth on Infrastructure

Urban growth significantly influences the development and efficiency of infrastructure within cities. This growth can lead to both challenges and opportunities in terms of managing and upgrading urban systems to sustain increasing populations.

### Challenges in Infrastructure Due to Urban Growth

With rapid urbanization, cities face multiple challenges in maintaining and developing infrastructure that meets the demands of growing populations. Some of these challenges include the strain on existing systems, such as transportation, water supply, and waste management.

Addressing these challenges requires [strategic planning](/explanations/architecture/urban-studies-in-architecture/strategic-planning/) and innovative solutions to prevent infrastructure from becoming overburdened due to rapid urban expansion.

**Example:** Delhi, India has faced significant challenges with water supply, where the rapid urban growth has led to increased demand that often exceeds available resources, prompting the need for external water sourcing and improved management systems.

### Mitigating Infrastructure Strain Through Urban Growth Management Strategies

Urban [growth management strategies](/explanations/architecture/urban-studies-in-architecture/growth-management-strategies/) are essential in mitigating the strain on infrastructure caused by urban expansion. These strategies assist in directing how and where growth occurs to prevent systems from becoming overwhelmed.

Through thoughtful management, cities can accommodate new growth while maintaining efficient infrastructure operations.

Utilizing data analytics can greatly enhance the effectiveness of urban growth management strategies by providing insights into infrastructure usage patterns.

### Leveraging Sustainable Architecture Practices for Infrastructure Resilience

Sustainable architecture practices contribute significantly to enhancing the resilience of [urban infrastructure](/explanations/architecture/urban-studies-in-architecture/urban-infrastructure/). By designing with sustainability in mind, cities can reduce resource consumption and bolster their infrastructure against future demands.

Adopting these practices not only supports [infrastructure resilience](/explanations/architecture/urban-studies-in-architecture/infrastructure-resilience/) but also fosters a more sustainable [urban environment](/explanations/architecture/urban-studies-in-architecture/urban-environment/) overall.

**Deep Dive:** The use of reflective materials in [building design](/explanations/architecture/real-estate/building-design/) can significantly decrease [urban heat islands](/explanations/architecture/urban-studies-in-architecture/urban-heat-islands/), thereby reducing the demand for [air conditioning](/explanations/architecture/mechanical-systems-in-architecture-design/air-conditioning/) and easing the strain on the electricity infrastructure.

## Managing Urban Growth with Sustainable Practices

Embracing sustainable practices is essential in managing urban growth. These practices not only focus on minimizing environmental impact but also on enhancing livability and resilience in urban areas.

### Integrating Sustainable Architecture Practices in Urban Planning

Sustainable architecture is a critical component of urban planning. By incorporating eco-friendly designs and materials, cities can reduce their carbon footprint and ensure resource-efficient development.

Through these innovative practices, urban areas can grow in a manner that supports environmental preservation and enhances the health and well-being of their inhabitants.

**Example:** The Bosco Verticale in Milan exemplifies sustainable architecture. This vertical forest reduces pollution, produces oxygen, and helps moderate building temperatures.

**Deep Dive:** The use of [vernacular architecture](/explanations/architecture/architecture-theory/vernacular-architecture/), which includes adapting local techniques and materials, can offer valuable insights into sustainable building practices tailored to specific climates. This approach utilizes the accumulated wisdom of local communities to create adaptive and resource-efficient structures.

### Advantages of Sustainable Practices in Urban Growth Management

The application of sustainable practices in urban growth management yields multiple benefits. These practices ensure the development of [urban spaces](/explanations/architecture/urban-design-in-architecture/urban-spaces/) that meet the demands of current and future populations without exhausting natural resources.

An approach that integrates these elements results in urban environments that are not only functional but also conducive to a higher quality of life for residents.

Did you know that cities implementing [green roofs](/explanations/architecture/environmental-design/green-roofs/) have witnessed up to a 75% reduction in stormwater runoff?

### Future Trends in Managing Urban Growth

Looking ahead, several emerging trends are set to [shape](/explanations/architecture/interior-design-in-architecture/shape/) the future of urban growth management. These include the integration of technology and innovative planning methods that focus on sustainability and efficiency.

By embracing these trends, cities can prepare for future challenges while ensuring sustainable and intelligent growth patterns.

**Deep Dive:** The future of urban growth is closely linked with the concept of circular cities, where waste is minimized, and materials are continuously repurposed to create a closed-loop system that mimics natural ecological processes. This ambitious approach requires rethinking city systems from production to consumption and waste management.

Sustainable urban development – strategies for modern cities
============================================================

With the advancement of globalization and industrialization, the process of urbanization has accelerated rapidly[1]. However, this process has brought multiple challenges such as resource depletion, environmental pollution, and social fragmentation. Sustainable urban development came into being with the aim of finding a way to support urban growth while protecting and enhancing environmental and social resources.

**Three****P****illars of****S****ustainable****U****rban****D****evelopment**
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Sustainable urban development is based on three pillars: economic, environmental and social sustainability[2].This holistic strategy emphasizes balanced development and aims to achieve long-term goals.

*   Economic sustainability: Economic growth is the basis for urban development. Sustainable economic strategies aim to promote innovation and competitiveness while ensuring that all community members benefit from them.
*   Environmental sustainability: Cities must take steps to protect natural resources, reduce pollution, improve energy efficiency, and implement strict environmental protection policies.
*   Social Sustainability: Enhance community cohesion and well-being and ensure equitable access to essential services such as education, health and safety.

**Key Strategies**
------------------

The realization of sustainable urban development relies on a number of key strategies.

*   Green infrastructure: Investing in green infrastructure such as public transportation and green buildings not only improves the quality of life for residents, but also helps reduce the environmental footprint.
*   Smart City Technologies: Leveraging smart technologies, such as IoT (Internet of Things) devices and smart data analytics, to optimize energy use, transportation and public services.
*   Sustainable urban planning: through scientific planning, rationally layout urban space, control urban expansion, protect the ecological environment, and improve land use efficiency.
*   Community participation and governance: Encourage citizens to participate in urban planning and enhance transparency and public participation in decision-making.

![Image 3: Image 2: Urban green infrastructure and sustainable development](https://nclurbandesign.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/2-768x329.png)

Image 1: Urban green infrastructure and sustainable development [https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/13/20/11498#](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/13/20/11498#)

**Precedents**
--------------

#### **Copenhagen, Denmark**

Copenhagen’s success as one of the world’s most sustainable cities lies in its comprehensive strategy, which includes large-scale investments in wind and other renewable energy sources, as well as its extensive network of cycle paths. In addition, Copenhagen is actively promoting green roofs and efficient waste recycling systems, with the goal of becoming the world’s first carbon-neutral capital by 2025[3].

![Image 4: Copenhagen city](https://nclurbandesign.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/1.png)

Image2: Copenhagen city [https://www.copcap.com/business-services](https://www.copcap.com/business-services)

#### **Bangkok, Thailand**

Faced with serious urban pollution and traffic problems, Bangkok has promoted green travel by improving the efficiency of its public transportation system, such as promoting electric buses and taxis. In addition, Bangkok has also made innovations in urban greening and water management systems, such as the establishment of multi-functional green spaces that can be used for public recreation and flood prevention during the rainy season.

**Conclusion**
--------------

Sustainable urban development requires a comprehensive, multi-field strategy that involves policy formulation, technological innovation, and broad social participation. It is not only a problem of technology and economy, but also a problem of culture and social structure. Successful urban sustainable development cases show that only when local governments, businesses, non-governmental organizations and residents can participate together and form synergy can truly sustainable development be achieved.

The future of cities depends on our choices and efforts today. In the long run, only those cities that can achieve a triple balance of economy, environment and society can maintain a leading position in global competition and provide residents with a high-quality living environment.

--------------

# Sustainable urban development

As places of home, work and leisure, cities play a pivotal role in people’s lives. While being a major contributor to GDP, they also account for large shares of energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions. Now more than ever, resilient, green and low-carbon cities are essential for an economically sound, socially responsible and environmentally sustainable future. Sustainable urban development policies seek to address a range of issues from managing urban expansion and congestion to fostering competitiveness, innovation, social inclusion and environmental sustainability - relevant to the achievement of SDG 11 on Sustainable Cities and Communities.

Policy sub-issue

Share

[Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.oecd.org%2Fen%2Ftopics%2Fsustainable-urban-development)
[Twitter](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.oecd.org%2Fen%2Ftopics%2Fsustainable-urban-development)
[LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/sharing/share-offsite/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.oecd.org%2Fen%2Ftopics%2Fsustainable-urban-development)

## Key messages

### 

Cities account for more than 70% of global energy-related CO2 emissions and an estimated 50% of global waste, and are home to nearly half of the world’s population. With a rising share of the global population expected to live in urban areas in the coming decades, the impact of cities on the environment – and their vulnerability to increasingly common extreme weather – will continue to grow unless significant action is scaled up.

Cities also provide concrete solutions to addressing climate change and to minimising wider impacts on the environment. Cities demonstrate strong leadership in many fields, setting sustainability and CO2 reduction targets that are often bolder than those of national governments. They are in close contact with citizens and businesses, are knowledgeable of local conditions and are well positioned to pilot ambitious measures in key environmental areas such as spatial planning, transport, buildings, and waste and water services. In the OECD, 60% of public investment occurs at the subnational level, of which nearly a third is channelled in to transport systems, underlying the key role of cities to invest in green and resilient urban infrastructure.

The OECD supports evidence-based policymaking that will drive the zero-carbon transition and secure sustainable and resilient cities and regions. We conduct policy analysis and share best practices in order to: i) craft actionable place-based policies; ii) improve coordination across levels of government; iii) amplify subnational governments’ significant, yet often overlooked, role in designing and implementing ambitious environmental policies.

### 

Sustainable urban development lies at the heart of the UN Sustainable Development Goals 11 on Sustainable Cities and Communities, which focuses on making cities and human settlements inclusive, safe, resilient and sustainable. In OECD countries, most local governments have a hand in policies that are central to sustainable urban development, including water, housing, transport, infrastructure and climate change and account for 55% of public investment. However, only 11% of regions and 17% of cities in the OECD have already achieved the suggested targets for SDG 11.

Using the framework of the SDGs can help reshape sustainable urban development policies, including by identifying place-based priorities, re-orient existing strategies and plans or shape new ones towards sustainable urban development, fostering vertical coordination across levels of government by promoting synergies among sectoral policies to overcome silos and fragmentation.

Learn more about OECD work on A Territorial Approach to the SDGs à Link to the new programme page of A Territorial Approach to the SDGs

## Context

### Compact urban development

Urban sprawl is increasing at a much faster pace than population growth, with the overall built-up area around the globe having increased 2.5 times over the last 40 years. Uncontrolled urban expansion creates negative impacts, such as higher infrastructure costs and diminished energy and resource efficiency. It can also result in encroachment on agricultural land, forests, open space or wetlands, with a corresponding loss of the economic, recreational and ecological values provided by those ecosystems. Longer commuting times without affordable public transport systems also diminishes access to jobs and services for many urban residents.

Compact urban form, characterised by dense and proximate development patterns linked by public transport systems and with accessibility to local services and jobs, can counteract such negative impacts and maximise the economic, social and environmental potential of cities. It is associated with a wide range of urban benefits, including increased productivity due to agglomeration economies, improved accessibility to urban services, reduced travel times, and a smaller ecological footprint due to lower energy and land consumption.

### Distance of cities (functional urban areas) in OECD countries that have achieved the suggested end values for SDG 11

The index for SDG 11 on sustainable cities combines two indicators, one on environmental quality and one on sustainable urbanisation, namely the average exposure to particulate matter 2.5 (PM2.5) and the difference between land consumption rate and population growth rate. Although only 11% of OECD regions have achieved the end values for SDG 11, 26% of OECD countries have at least one region that has met the expected objectives. Compared to regions, cities are performing better in the indicators of SDG 11. Out of 637 cities, 110 (17%) have achieved the end values of both exposure to air pollution lower than 10 µg/m³ and an equal growth rate in land consumption and population.

## Related policy issues

* [Inclusive growth in cities](/en/topics/inclusive-growth-in-cities.html)

  Cities are hubs of economic growth, opportunity, and diversity, but are also home to wide inequalities. Our work on inclusive growth in cities examines how national and local governments can foster economic growth in cities that is distributed fairly and creates opportunities for all.

  Learn more
* [Urban housing](/en/topics/urban-housing.html)

  Along with greater opportunity, city life has increasingly meant housing unaffordability, substandard quality, and overcrowding. As the world grows more urban, governments must find creative ways to provide affordable, quality, and sustainable housing for all.

  Learn more

enriched_points.expanded_content: Implementing sustainable urban planning practices is crucial for maintaining ecological balance, optimizing resource use, and enhancing the quality of life in growing urban areas.
Strategies must be developed to bridge gaps between different socio-economic groups, ensuring equitable access to services, opportunities, and resources that foster inclusive urban development.
Migration can be harnessed as a catalyst for urban growth, bringing diverse skills and innovation that can enhance economic vitality and cultural richness in cities.
